narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 54% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your academic record, including being in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack ranking. Your LSAT score of 31 also falls well below the average of about 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 is lower than the usual 3.2. You're from a middle-income family, identify as female and White, and plan to attend part-time, which is less common than full-time enrollment. When the model weighed these factors, your third-year decile rank had the biggest impact, strongly nudging the prediction toward expecting a bar failure due to that low performance. Right behind it, your LSAT score reinforced that concern by signaling potential challenges ahead. Your first-year decile came next in influence, similarly highlighting early struggles compared to peers. Family income played a moderate role, followed by your racial background, undergraduate GPA, part-time plans, and gender, with each one contributing in turn to the overall risk assessment—none pulled strongly in the positive direction for you. In essence, your profile stands out from typical admitted students because of these below-average academic markers, especially the consistent low decile rankings that suggest hurdles in sustaining strong performance. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT to aim higher than 37, build better study habits proven to help bar prep, and consider full-time enrollment if feasible to match successful peers. With targeted improvements like these, you could shift the model's view and open new doors—many students turn things around with persistence. Keep pushing; your determination will make a difference.
